title: Go Output
content:
### Printing Output in Go

The `fmt` package provides formatted I/O functions.

### Print vs Println vs Printf

```go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Print("No newline")
    fmt.Println("With newline")
    fmt.Printf("Formatted: %s, %d\n", "hello", 42)
}
```

| Function | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `fmt.Print()` | Prints without newline |
| `fmt.Println()` | Prints with newline |
| `fmt.Printf()` | Formatted printing |

### Formatting Verbs

| Verb | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `%v` | Default value format |
| `%+v` | Struct field names + values |
| `%#v` | Go-syntax representation |
| `%T` | Type of the value |
| `%d` | Decimal integer |
| `%s` | String |
| `%f` | Float |
| `%t` | Boolean |
| `%b` | Binary |
| `%x` | Hexadecimal |

### Examples

```go
name := "Alice"
age := 30
fmt.Printf("Name: %s, Age: %d\n", name, age)
fmt.Printf("Type: %T, Value: %v\n", name, name)
```

### Sprintf

Returns a formatted string without printing:

```go
result := fmt.Sprintf("%s is %d years old", name, age)
```

> **Key Point:** `fmt.Printf` uses format verbs similar to C's printf but with Go-specific additions like `%v` and `%#v`.
codeExample:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    name := "Go"
    year := 2009
    fmt.Println("Hello,", name)
    fmt.Printf("%s was created in %d\n", name, year)
    fmt.Printf("Value: %v, Type: %T\n", year, year)
}

quiz.question: Which function prints with a newline?
What verb prints the type of a value?
